sentiment.rationale: The creation account declares the power and glory of God as He creates the heavens and the earth, pronouncing each step good.
God rests from creation, establishes the Garden of Eden, and institutes marriage — a chapter of harmony and divine order.
The Fall of Adam and Eve brings divine judgment, curses, and expulsion from the Garden, though with a promise of redemption.
Cain murders Abel, receives God's curse, and a lineage of violence follows through Lamech's boast.
A genealogical record from Adam to Noah with the recurring refrain of birth and death, and the notable translation of Enoch.
Widespread wickedness grieves God, leading to the decision to destroy the earth by flood; only Noah finds grace.
The Flood destroys all flesh outside the ark as divine judgment falls upon a corrupt world.
The waters recede, Noah's ark rests on Ararat, and God accepts Noah's burnt offering — a new beginning emerges.
God establishes the Noahic covenant with the rainbow, blesses Noah's family, but the chapter ends with Ham's transgression and Noah's curse.
The Table of Nations traces the descendants of Shem, Ham, and Japheth spreading across the earth after the Flood.
The Tower of Babel represents human pride met with divine scattering, followed by the genealogy from Shem to Abram.
God calls Abram with magnificent promises of land and posterity, though Abram's deception in Egypt introduces tension.
Abram and Lot separate peacefully, and God reaffirms His covenant promises of land and posterity to Abram.
Abram rescues Lot by military victory, and Melchizedek blesses Abram and receives tithes — a type of Christ's priesthood.
God makes a formal covenant with Abram, promising innumerable posterity and the land; Abram's faith is counted as righteousness.
Sarai's plan for Hagar leads to conflict and Hagar's flight into the wilderness, though God promises to multiply her seed.
God establishes the covenant of circumcision, renames Abram to Abraham and Sarai to Sarah, and promises Isaac's birth.
Three heavenly visitors confirm Sarah's coming son, followed by Abraham's intercession for Sodom — a mix of promise and impending judgment.
The destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah is a severe divine judgment, with Lot's wife turned to salt and his daughters' transgression.
Abraham deceives Abimelech regarding Sarah, prompting God's warning to Abimelech and eventual resolution.
Isaac is born fulfilling the covenant promise; Hagar and Ishmael are sent away but preserved by God; Abraham and Abimelech make peace.
The binding of Isaac is the supreme test of faith, with God providing a ram and reaffirming the covenant blessings — a profound type of the Atonement.
Sarah dies and Abraham negotiates the purchase of the cave of Machpelah — an honorable and peaceful transaction.
Abraham's servant is guided by divine providence to find Rebekah as Isaac's bride — a beautiful account of faith and answered prayer.
Abraham dies; Esau sells his birthright to Jacob, foreshadowing future conflict over the covenant blessings.
God reaffirms the Abrahamic covenant with Isaac, who prospers greatly despite contention over wells.
Jacob deceives Isaac to obtain the birthright blessing; Esau's bitter anguish and murderous intent drive Jacob's departure.
Jacob's vision at Bethel — the ladder reaching to heaven with angels ascending and descending — and God's covenant promise renewed.
Jacob arrives at Laban's house, marries Leah and Rachel after Laban's deception, and the birth of sons begins amidst marital rivalry.
The birth rivalry between Rachel and Leah continues; Jacob prospers through his livestock-breeding arrangement with Laban.
Jacob flees from Laban; mutual accusations and a tense pursuit end with a covenant at Mizpah.
Jacob wrestles with the angel and is renamed Israel, a profound spiritual transformation before reuniting with Esau.
Jacob and Esau reconcile with tears and embrace — a scene of forgiveness and restored brotherhood.
The defilement of Dinah and the violent revenge of Simeon and Levi bring shame and danger upon Jacob's household.
Jacob returns to Bethel, purifies his household, and God reaffirms the covenant; Rachel dies giving birth to Benjamin.
A genealogical record of Esau's descendants and the chiefs and kings of Edom.
Joseph's brothers sell him into slavery out of jealousy; Jacob mourns, believing Joseph is dead.
Judah's story with Tamar involves deception, broken promises, and the birth of Perez and Zerah — a complex moral narrative.
Joseph prospers in Potiphar's house by divine favor, but is falsely accused and imprisoned — his faithfulness persists under trial.
Joseph interprets the dreams of the butler and baker in prison — one restored, one executed.
Pharaoh's dreams interpreted by Joseph lead to his dramatic rise to power over all Egypt — a triumph of divine providence.
Joseph's brothers come to Egypt and do not recognize him; their guilt over selling Joseph surfaces as they face adversity.
The brothers return to Egypt with Benjamin; Joseph hosts a meal and is moved to tears seeing his youngest brother.
Joseph tests his brothers with the silver cup; Judah's impassioned plea to take Benjamin's place shows repentance and transformation.
Joseph reveals himself to his brothers, weeping, and attributes all events to God's providence — a climactic scene of reconciliation and grace.
God reassures Jacob in a vision; Israel travels to Egypt and the family is reunited with Joseph in Goshen.
Jacob's family settles in Goshen; Joseph administers Egypt through the famine; Jacob nears death and makes Joseph swear to bury him in Canaan.
Jacob blesses Ephraim and Manasseh, crossing his hands to give the greater blessing to the younger — a prophetic act of faith.
Jacob's prophetic blessings over his twelve sons contain both glorious promises (Judah's scepter, Joseph's blessings) and stern rebukes.
Jacob is buried in Canaan; Joseph reassures his brothers of forgiveness, affirming God's providence, and dies in Egypt at 110.

summaries.summary: God creates the heavens and earth in six days — light, sky, land, vegetation, sun and moon, sea creatures, animals, and mankind in His image — and declares all of it very good.
God rests on the seventh day, forms Adam from the dust and places him in the Garden of Eden, then creates Eve from Adam's rib as his companion.
The serpent deceives Eve; Adam and Eve eat the forbidden fruit, receive curses and consequences, and are cast out of the Garden of Eden.
Cain kills his brother Abel out of jealousy over God's acceptance of Abel's offering; Cain is cursed to be a fugitive, and the chapter traces his descendants.
The genealogical record from Adam to Noah, noting the long lifespans of the patriarchs and the translation of Enoch who walked with God.
Wickedness fills the earth and God determines to destroy all flesh by a flood; Noah finds grace and is commanded to build an ark.
Noah, his family, and the animals enter the ark; the Flood covers the earth for forty days and all living things outside the ark perish.
The floodwaters recede, the ark rests on Mount Ararat, Noah sends out a raven and a dove, and upon leaving the ark he offers a burnt offering to God.
God blesses Noah and establishes the rainbow covenant never to flood the earth again; Noah plants a vineyard, and Ham's transgression leads to the cursing of Canaan.
The Table of Nations records the descendants of Shem, Ham, and Japheth and how the nations were divided across the earth after the Flood.
The people build the Tower of Babel and God confounds their language and scatters them; the genealogy from Shem to Abram is recorded.
God calls Abram to leave his country with promises of a great nation and blessing; Abram journeys to Canaan and then to Egypt, where he deceives Pharaoh about Sarai.
Abram and Lot separate due to the size of their herds; Lot chooses the plain of Jordan, and God reaffirms His covenant promises to Abram.
Abram rescues Lot from captivity after the battle of the kings, and Melchizedek, king of Salem and priest of the most high God, blesses Abram and receives tithes.
God makes a formal covenant with Abram, promising him innumerable posterity and the land of Canaan; Abram believes God and it is counted to him for righteousness.
Sarai gives her handmaid Hagar to Abram; Hagar conceives and flees from Sarai's harshness, but the angel of the Lord sends her back with a promise about Ishmael.
God establishes the covenant of circumcision, renames Abram to Abraham and Sarai to Sarah, and promises that Sarah will bear a son named Isaac.
Three heavenly visitors announce the birth of Isaac to Abraham and Sarah; Abraham intercedes with God for the city of Sodom.
Two angels rescue Lot from Sodom before God destroys Sodom and Gomorrah with fire and brimstone; Lot's wife looks back and becomes a pillar of salt.
Abraham sojourns in Gerar and tells Abimelech that Sarah is his sister; God warns Abimelech in a dream and the matter is resolved.
Isaac is born to Abraham and Sarah; Hagar and Ishmael are sent away but preserved by God; Abraham and Abimelech make a covenant at Beer-sheba.
God commands Abraham to offer Isaac as a sacrifice; Abraham obeys, and at the last moment God provides a ram and reaffirms the covenant blessings.
Sarah dies at age 127 and Abraham purchases the cave of Machpelah from Ephron the Hittite as a burial place for her.
Abraham sends his servant to find a wife for Isaac; the servant is divinely guided to Rebekah, who returns with him and marries Isaac.
Abraham dies and is buried at Machpelah; Ishmael's descendants are listed; Rebekah bears twins Esau and Jacob, and Esau sells his birthright.
God reaffirms the Abrahamic covenant with Isaac; Isaac prospers in Gerar despite disputes over wells and makes a covenant with Abimelech.
Jacob, guided by Rebekah, deceives the aging Isaac and receives the firstborn blessing; Esau is filled with hatred and plans to kill Jacob.
Isaac sends Jacob to Laban; at Bethel Jacob dreams of a ladder reaching to heaven with angels, and God renews the Abrahamic covenant with him.
Jacob arrives at Laban's household, works seven years for Rachel but is given Leah first; he marries Rachel also, and Leah begins bearing sons.
The birth rivalry between Rachel and Leah continues through handmaids and mandrakes; Jacob prospers through a livestock agreement with Laban.
Jacob flees from Laban with his family and flocks; Laban pursues, and after a tense confrontation they make a covenant at Mizpah.
Jacob prepares to meet Esau, sends gifts ahead, and wrestles with a divine being through the night, receiving the new name Israel.
Jacob and Esau meet and reconcile peacefully; Jacob settles near Shechem and builds an altar.
Shechem defiles Dinah; Simeon and Levi avenge her by deceiving and slaughtering the men of the city, bringing trouble upon Jacob's household.
God directs Jacob to return to Bethel where the covenant is reaffirmed; Rachel dies giving birth to Benjamin, and Isaac dies at age 180.
The genealogical record of Esau's descendants and the chiefs and kings of Edom who settled in Mount Seir.
Joseph reports his prophetic dreams to his family; his jealous brothers sell him to Midianite traders, and he is taken to Egypt.
Judah's son Er dies and his brother Onan refuses his duty; Tamar disguises herself and conceives twins Perez and Zerah by Judah.
Joseph prospers in the house of Potiphar by God's blessing but is falsely accused by Potiphar's wife and imprisoned.
Joseph correctly interprets the dreams of Pharaoh's butler and baker while in prison; the butler is restored but forgets Joseph.
Joseph interprets Pharaoh's dreams of seven years of plenty followed by seven years of famine and is elevated to rule over all Egypt.
Joseph's brothers come to Egypt to buy grain; Joseph recognizes them, tests them, and keeps Simeon as hostage until they bring Benjamin.
The brothers return to Egypt with Benjamin; Joseph hosts them at a feast and is overcome with emotion at seeing his youngest brother.
Joseph plants his silver cup in Benjamin's sack to test his brothers; Judah delivers an impassioned plea offering himself in Benjamin's place.
Joseph reveals his identity to his brothers, weeps with them, and sends them to bring Jacob to Egypt, declaring God sent him ahead to preserve life.
God reassures Jacob in a vision at Beer-sheba; Jacob's entire family travels to Egypt and is reunited with Joseph in Goshen.
Jacob's family settles in Goshen; Joseph administers Egypt through the famine; Jacob makes Joseph swear to bury him in the land of Canaan.
Jacob adopts and blesses Joseph's sons Ephraim and Manasseh, deliberately crossing his hands to give the greater blessing to the younger Ephraim.
Jacob gathers his twelve sons and delivers prophetic blessings and pronouncements over each tribe, including the messianic promise to Judah.
Jacob is embalmed and buried in the cave of Machpelah; Joseph reassures his brothers of forgiveness, and Joseph dies in Egypt at age 110.

crossReferences.note: Both passages establish God as the Creator of all things from the beginning.
The Pearl of Great Price provides a parallel creation account with additional detail.
Jesus quotes this verse on the divine institution of marriage.
The protevangelium connects to the eschatological defeat of the serpent.
The Pearl of Great Price provides a parallel account of the curse upon Adam.
Both passages address the progression of sin when temptation is entertained.
The New Testament explains that Enoch was translated because he pleased God.
Peter references the ark as a type of salvation through water.
The JST expands the rainbow covenant with additional context about the last days.
Paul quotes the Abrahamic covenant promise that all nations will be blessed.
Melchizedek's priesthood is a type of Christ's eternal priesthood.
Paul quotes this verse as the foundational text on justification by faith.
Paul references the renaming of Abraham as father of many nations.
The binding of Isaac is cited as Abraham's supreme act of faith.
Peter quotes this Abrahamic covenant promise regarding the Messiah.
Jesus references Jacob's ladder as a type of Himself as the connection between heaven and earth.
The scepter of Judah and Shiloh prophecy find fulfillment in Christ, the Lion of Judah.
Joseph's declaration that God meant evil for good parallels Paul's teaching on divine providence.

verses.reason: The foundational opening of the Bible establishing God as Creator.
God created man in his own image — foundational to the doctrine of divine nature.
The institution of marriage — quoted by Jesus, Paul, and in temple theology.
The protevangelium — the first messianic prophecy promising the seed of the woman will crush the serpent.
'Dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return' — the mortality of mankind.
Cain's infamous reply: 'Am I my brother's keeper?' — a universal question of moral responsibility.
'Enoch walked with God: and he was not; for God took him' — the translation of Enoch.
'But Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD' — the first mention of grace in scripture.
God's promise that seedtime, harvest, and the seasons will never cease.
The rainbow covenant — God's sign that He will never again destroy the earth by flood.
The foundational Abrahamic covenant promise: 'I will make of thee a great nation.'
'In thee shall all families of the earth be blessed' — the universal scope of the Abrahamic covenant.
The introduction of Melchizedek, king of Salem and priest of the most high God — a type of Christ.
'He believed in the LORD; and he counted it to him for righteousness' — the paradigm of faith.
The renaming of Abram to Abraham — 'father of many nations.'
'Is any thing too hard for the LORD?' — a declaration of God's unlimited power.
'God will provide himself a lamb' — Abraham's prophetic statement of the Atonement.
Abraham names the place Jehovah-jireh — 'The LORD will provide.'
Jacob's ladder — a vision of the connection between heaven and earth with ascending and descending angels.
'This is none other but the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven.'
The Mizpah benediction: 'The LORD watch between me and thee, when we are absent one from another.'
Jacob receives the name Israel — 'for as a prince hast thou power with God and with men.'
The brothers call Joseph 'this dreamer' — encapsulating the jealousy that drives the Joseph narrative.
Joseph's refusal of Potiphar's wife: 'How then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?'
Joseph credits God, not himself, for the interpretation of dreams.
Judah offers himself as a slave in Benjamin's place — a powerful act of self-sacrifice.
Joseph tells his brothers God sent him ahead to preserve life — the theology of divine providence.
Jacob invokes 'the Angel which redeemed me' to bless Ephraim and Manasseh.
The Shiloh prophecy — 'The sceptre shall not depart from Judah' — a key messianic text.
Joseph is a fruitful bough — the basis for LDS teachings on Joseph's posterity in the Americas.
'Ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good' — the definitive statement of divine providence.
